content:
# Leviticus Chapter 16 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 67v–68r
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 102–103
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/LEV_016.md`

---

## Council Notes

Leviticus 16 is the theological center of the entire book and one of the most architecturally intricate chapters in the Torah. It legislates Yom ha-Kippurim — the Day of Atonements — the single annual occasion on which the high priest enters the innermost chamber of the Tabernacle, the Holy of Holies (qodesh ha-qodashim), to make atonement for the sanctuary itself, for the priestly house, and for the entire congregation of Israel. The chapter is introduced by a narrative hook: "after the death of the two sons of Aaron" (v. 1), linking the legislation directly to the catastrophe of Leviticus 10:1–2, where Nadab and Abihu died for unauthorized entry into the divine Presence. The entire elaborate protocol of Leviticus 16 is the answer to that death: *this* is how, and *only* how, one may approach the LORD's most intimate dwelling.

The chapter's structural logic moves concentrically outward from the innermost sacred space: the Holy of Holies (vv. 11–14) → the outer sanctuary/tent of meeting (vv. 15–17) → the altar of burnt offering (vv. 18–19) → the people/camp (vv. 20–22) → outside the camp entirely (vv. 27–28). The atonement mirrors the concentric holiness geography of the Tabernacle: the blood must penetrate all the way to the center, because the defilement has penetrated all the way in.

The chapter's most theologically singular feature is the two-goat ritual (vv. 7–10, 20–22). Two animals accomplish together what neither can accomplish alone: the first goat is slaughtered and its blood taken behind the veil — a death atonement that addresses the sanctuary's contamination. The second goat is not slaughtered; it is confessed over, laden with Israel's sins by Aaron's two-handed laying-on, and sent alive into the wilderness to Azazel — a removal atonement that addresses the people's moral burden. One theology, two dramatic acts: substitutionary death and complete removal.

**Azazel** (vv. 8, 10, 26): preserved untranslated throughout, per project ruling. The term is among the most contested in biblical Hebrew — interpretations range from a wilderness demon or fallen angel (1 Enoch), to a place name ("rugged terrain"), to an abstract concept ("complete removal"). The syntactic structure of v. 8 places Azazel in grammatical parallel with YHWH ("one lot for the LORD and one lot for Azazel"), which the text does not resolve. All three agents preserve Azazel untranslated; this is not a disagreement point but a project convention confirmed.

**Kapporet** (vv. 2, 13, 14, 15): preserved untranslated with bracketed gloss [atonement cover] on first occurrence. The kapporet — the gold lid of the ark of the covenant, flanked by the cherubim — is where YHWH appears in the cloud (v. 2) and where the blood of atonement is applied (v. 14). The LXX renders it ἱλαστήριον (hilasterion, "mercy seat / propitiatory"), the term Paul applies to Christ in Rom 3:25. The project preserves the Hebrew term precisely because its English equivalents ("mercy seat," "atonement cover") each capture only part of the word's semantic range.

**Shabbat shabbaton** (v. 31): the doubled intensive noun — "a sabbath of solemn rest" — is the highest grade of Sabbath observance in the Levitical calendar, used only for Yom Kippur and the weekly Sabbath. The transliteration is retained in brackets on first occurrence.

**Ve-innitem et-nafshoteikhem** (vv. 29, 31) — "you shall afflict yourselves": the Piel of anah with the reflexive object. The rendering preserves the full semantic range: fasting, yes, but also broadly self-humbling. "Humble yourselves" (PNEUMA) captures the interior dimension; "afflict yourselves" (EMET, LOGOS) captures the bodily-discipline dimension. The consensus adopts "afflict yourselves" as the more specific formal rendering that encompasses what PNEUMA's "humble" also means.

**Key term rulings:** chattat = "sin offering" | olah = "burnt offering" | YHWH = "the LORD" | bney yisrael = "sons of Israel" | Azazel = preserved untranslated | kapporet = preserved with gloss | shabbat shabbaton = "sabbath of solemn rest [shabbat shabbaton]"

---

## Consensus Translation

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses after the death of the two sons of Aaron, when they drew near before the LORD and died.

**2** And the LORD said to Moses: "Speak to Aaron your brother, that he shall not come at any time into the Holy Place, within the veil, before the kapporet [atonement cover] that is upon the ark — lest he die. For I will appear in the cloud over the kapporet.

**3** "But with this shall Aaron come into the Holy Place: with a bull as a sin offering and a ram as a burnt offering.

**4** He shall put on the holy linen tunic, and linen undergarments shall be upon his body, and with a linen sash shall he gird himself, and a linen turban shall he put upon his head. These are the holy garments. He shall bathe his body in water and put them on.

**5** "And from the congregation of the sons of Israel he shall take two male goats as a sin offering and one ram as a burnt offering.

**6** "And Aaron shall present the bull as the sin offering that is for himself, and make atonement for himself and for his household.

**7** Then he shall take the two goats and set them before the LORD at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

**8** And Aaron shall cast lots over the two goats — one lot for the LORD and one lot for Azazel.

**9** And Aaron shall present the goat on which the lot fell for the LORD, and offer it as a sin offering.

**10** But the goat on which the lot fell for Azazel shall be stood alive before the LORD, to make atonement over it, by sending it away to Azazel into the wilderness.

**11** "And Aaron shall present the bull as the sin offering that is for himself, and he shall make atonement for himself and for his household. He shall slaughter the bull as the sin offering that is for himself.

**12** And he shall take a censer full of coals of fire from the altar before the LORD, and his hands full of finely ground fragrant incense, and bring them inside the veil.

**13** He shall put the incense on the fire before the LORD, so that the cloud of the incense covers the kapporet that is over the testimony — lest he die.

**14** And he shall take some of the blood of the bull and sprinkle it with his finger on the front of the kapporet, toward the east; and before the kapporet he shall sprinkle some of the blood with his finger seven times.

**15** "Then he shall slaughter the goat of the sin offering that is for the people and bring its blood inside the veil. He shall do with its blood as he did with the blood of the bull: he shall sprinkle it on the kapporet and before the kapporet.

**16** Thus he shall make atonement for the Holy Place, because of the uncleannesses of the sons of Israel and because of their transgressions, all their sins. And so he shall do for the tent of meeting, which dwells with them in the midst of their uncleannesses.

**17** No man shall be in the tent of meeting when he enters to make atonement in the Holy Place, until he comes out and has made atonement for himself and for his household and for all the congregation of Israel.

**18** "Then he shall go out to the altar that is before the LORD and make atonement for it. He shall take some of the blood of the bull and some of the blood of the goat and put it on the horns of the altar all around.

**19** And he shall sprinkle some of the blood on it with his finger seven times, and cleanse it and consecrate it from the uncleannesses of the sons of Israel.

**20** "And when he has finished making atonement for the Holy Place and the tent of meeting and the altar, he shall present the live goat.

**21** And Aaron shall lay both his hands on the head of the live goat and confess over it all the iniquities of the sons of Israel, and all their transgressions, all their sins. He shall put them on the head of the goat and send it away into the wilderness by the hand of a man who is in readiness.

**22** The goat shall bear on itself all their iniquities to a land cut off, and he shall release the goat in the wilderness.

**23** "Then Aaron shall come into the tent of meeting and shall take off the linen garments that he put on when he went into the Holy Place, and shall leave them there.

**24** He shall bathe his body in water in a holy place and put on his garments, and come out and offer his burnt offering and the burnt offering of the people, and make atonement for himself and for the people.

**25** And the fat of the sin offering he shall burn on the altar.

**26** "And the one who sends away the goat for Azazel shall wash his clothes and bathe his body in water, and afterward he may come into the camp.

**27** And the bull for the sin offering and the goat for the sin offering, whose blood was brought in to make atonement in the Holy Place, shall be carried outside the camp. Their skin and their flesh and their dung shall be burned with fire.

**28** And the one who burns them shall wash his clothes and bathe his body in water, and afterward he may come into the camp.

**29** "And it shall be a statute forever for you: in the seventh month, on the tenth of the month, you shall afflict yourselves and shall do no work — the native and the sojourner who sojourns among you.

**30** For on this day atonement shall be made for you, to cleanse you. From all your sins before the LORD you shall be clean.

**31** It is a sabbath of solemn rest [shabbat shabbaton] to you, and you shall afflict yourselves. It is a statute forever.

**32** And the priest who is anointed and consecrated to serve as priest in his father's place shall make atonement, wearing the holy linen garments.

**33** He shall make atonement for the holy sanctuary, and he shall make atonement for the tent of meeting and for the altar, and he shall make atonement for the priests and for all the people of the assembly.

**34** And this shall be a statute forever for you, that atonement may be made for the sons of Israel once in the year because of all their sins." And Moses did as the LORD commanded him.

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V. 1 — Name forms.** EMET: "Moshe," "Aharon." LOGOS: "Moses," "Aaron." PNEUMA: "Moses," "Aaron." Consensus: "Moses," "Aaron" — English name forms throughout, per project convention (established from Genesis onward).

**V. 1 — "spoke" vs. "said."** EMET: "spoke" (vayedabber). LOGOS: "spoke." PNEUMA: "spoke." All three agree on "spoke" for v. 1. Note: v. 2 opens with a second verb, vayomer ("said"), which is correctly distinguished as "said" in LOGOS and PNEUMA and which the consensus likewise distinguishes.

**V. 1 — "the LORD's presence" (PNEUMA).** PNEUMA paraphrases: "the ones who came near the LORD's presence." EMET and LOGOS: "before the LORD." Hebrew: lifnei YHWH. Consensus: "before the LORD" — the formal phrase lifnei YHWH should be preserved as a technical cultic expression; "the LORD's presence" is accurate but overly interpretive.

**V. 2 — "Speak" vs. "Tell."** EMET: "Speak" (dabber). LOGOS: "Speak." PNEUMA: "Tell." Consensus: "Speak" — the Piel imperative of dabar (dabber) is the standard prophetic-legal speech formula throughout Leviticus; "tell" (PNEUMA) is too casual for the register.

**V. 2 — "at any time" vs. "at all times" vs. "whenever he chooses."** EMET: "at all times." LOGOS: "at any time." PNEUMA: "whenever he chooses." Hebrew: be-khol-et ("in/at every time"). Consensus: "at any time" — LOGOS's rendering is idiomatically cleaner than EMET's "at all times" and more accurate than PNEUMA's interpretive expansion "whenever he chooses."

**V. 2 — kapporet rendering.** EMET: "kapporet" (untranslated, no gloss). LOGOS: "kapporet [atonement cover]." PNEUMA: "mercy seat." Consensus: "kapporet [atonement cover]" — project ruling preserves the Hebrew term with bracketed gloss on first occurrence; "mercy seat" (PNEUMA) is a legitimate rendering (KJV tradition, Luther's Gnadenstuhl) but adopts one tradition's interpretive emphasis; the project preserves both dimensions through transliteration + gloss.

**V. 2 — "Most Holy Place" vs. "Holy Place" (PNEUMA, v. 2).** PNEUMA uses "Most Holy Place" in v. 2. EMET and LOGOS: "the holy place" (ha-qodesh). The MT in v. 2 uses ha-qodesh alone; the full qodesh ha-qodashim ("Holy of Holies") does not appear until v. 33 (miqdashe ha-qodesh). Context makes clear the inner chamber is meant, but the text's own distinction between ha-qodesh ("the Holy Place") and qodesh ha-qodashim ("the Holy of Holies") should be preserved. Consensus: "the Holy Place" in v. 2, reserving "Holy of Holies" for v. 33. PNEUMA's "Most Holy Place" throughout conflates the two.

**V. 2 — "lest he die" vs. "or he will die."** EMET: "that he not die." LOGOS: "lest he die." PNEUMA: "or he will die." Hebrew: ve-lo' yamut. Consensus: "lest he die" — the purpose clause is well-captured by "lest"; PNEUMA's conditional "or he will die" imports a conditionality that is stronger than necessary.

**V. 3 — "With this" vs. "But thus" vs. "This is how."** EMET: "With this." LOGOS: "But thus." PNEUMA: "This is how." Hebrew: be-zot ("with/in this"). Consensus: "But with this" — combines the adversative contrast of LOGOS (signaling a pivot from the prohibition of v. 2 to the permitted approach) with the more literal be-zot phrasing of EMET. PNEUMA's paraphrase "This is how" is readable but loses the contrastive force.

**V. 3 — "a bull" vs. "a young bull."** EMET: "a bull." LOGOS: "a bull from the herd." PNEUMA: "a young bull." Hebrew: par ben-baqar ("a bull, son of the herd"). Consensus: "a bull" — the standard translation; "from the herd" (LOGOS) adds the qualifier ben-baqar explicitly; "young bull" (PNEUMA) adds an age qualifier not in the MT. Consensus: "a bull" is concise; the "from the herd" qualifier is absorbed into context.

**V. 4 — Garment order: "undergarments" / "breeches."** EMET: "linen breeches." LOGOS: "linen undergarments." PNEUMA: "linen undergarments." Hebrew: mikhnesei-bad ("breeches/trousers of linen"). Consensus: "linen undergarments" — "breeches" (EMET) is accurate but archaic; "undergarments" (LOGOS, PNEUMA) is clearer in contemporary English while conveying the sense of inner garment.

**V. 4 — Garment order: turban.** EMET: "a linen turban shall he wrap his head." LOGOS: "he shall bind the linen turban on his head." PNEUMA: "wrap the linen turban around his head." Hebrew: mitznefet-bad yitznof al-rosho. Consensus: "a linen turban he shall put upon his head" — slight rewording for clarity; the verb yitznof means "to wrap/bind"; "put upon" captures the action without archaism.

**V. 4 — "sacred" vs. "holy."** PNEUMA uses "sacred garments" throughout. EMET and LOGOS: "holy garments." Hebrew: bigdei qodesh. Consensus: "holy garments" — "holy" is the standard rendering of qodesh in Leviticus throughout this translation series; "sacred" (PNEUMA) is a near-synonym but inconsistent with the established project vocabulary.

**V. 4 — Bathing instruction placement.** EMET and LOGOS place the bathing instruction at the end of the verse ("He shall bathe his body in water and put them on"). PNEUMA inverts the order: "Before putting them on, he shall bathe his whole body in water." The Hebrew order matches EMET/LOGOS. Consensus follows the Hebrew order.

**V. 5 — "congregation" vs. "community."** EMET: "congregation of the sons of Israel." LOGOS: "congregation of the sons of Israel." PNEUMA: "whole community of Israel." Hebrew: adat bney yisrael. Consensus: "congregation of the sons of Israel" — "congregation" (EMET, LOGOS) is the standard rendering for adat ("assembly/congregation") throughout Leviticus; PNEUMA's "community" is acceptable but inconsistent with established project vocabulary. PNEUMA also drops "sons of" (bney), reducing bney yisrael to "Israel."

**V. 5 — "take" vs. "receive."** EMET: "he shall take." LOGOS: "he shall take." PNEUMA: "he shall receive." Hebrew: yiqqach ("he shall take"). Consensus: "he shall take" — yiqqach is the standard verb for taking/procuring; "receive" implies a passive handover not specified in the Hebrew.

**V. 6 — "bring near" / "offer" / "bring."** EMET: "Aharon shall bring near." LOGOS: "Aaron shall offer." PNEUMA: "Aaron shall bring." Hebrew: ve-hiqriv Aharon (hiphil of qarav, "to cause to draw near, to present"). Consensus: "Aaron shall present" — "present" captures the technical sacrificial sense of hiqriv without the archaism of "bring near" (EMET) or the interpretive compression of "offer" (LOGOS).

**V. 7 — "set them" vs. "stand them."** EMET: "he shall stand them before the LORD." LOGOS: "set them before the LORD." PNEUMA: "present them before the LORD." Hebrew: ve-he'emid otam lifnei YHWH (hiphil of 'amad: "to cause to stand, to station"). Consensus: "set them before the LORD" — "set" is natural English; "stand them" (EMET) is literal but sounds odd in English; "present" (PNEUMA) is too near the v. 6 verb.

**V. 8 — "cast lots" — verb for natan.** EMET: "shall cast lots" (EMET uses the literal "shall place"). LOGOS: "shall cast lots." PNEUMA: "shall cast lots." All three agree on "cast lots" functionally; EMET's "place" is the literal verb but "cast lots" is idiomatic. Consensus: "cast lots."

**V. 10 — "to make atonement over it, by sending it away" vs. compound syntax.** EMET: "to make atonement over it — to send it away as Azazel." LOGOS: "to make atonement over it by sending it away to Azazel into the wilderness." PNEUMA: "to make atonement over it, and then sent off into the wilderness to Azazel." Consensus follows LOGOS's smooth syntax; the atonement is made by the act of sending, and LOGOS captures this connection clearly.

**V. 12 — "hands full" vs. "two handfuls."** EMET: "his hands full." LOGOS: "his hands full." PNEUMA: "two handfuls." Hebrew: melo' hofnav ("fulness of his palms / his palms full"). Consensus: "his hands full" — melo' hofnav is a construct phrase ("fullness of his hands"), not specifically "two handfuls"; PNEUMA's "two handfuls" is functionally equivalent but adds the number explicitly; "his hands full" is more literal and natural.

**V. 12 — "coals of fire" vs. "burning coals."** EMET: "fiery coals." LOGOS: "coals of fire." PNEUMA: "burning coals." Hebrew: gachalei esh ("coals of fire"). Consensus: "coals of fire" — LOGOS preserves the construct phrase literally.

**V. 12 — "inside the veil" vs. "behind the curtain."** EMET: "from within to the veil." LOGOS: "inside the veil." PNEUMA: "behind the curtain." Hebrew: mi-beit la-parokhet ("from inside the veil / within the veil"). Consensus: "inside the veil" — LOGOS is the clearest; "behind the curtain" (PNEUMA) is accurate but parokhet is the standard "veil" of the Tabernacle literature, not merely a "curtain."

**V. 13 — "testimony" vs. "covenant tablets."** EMET: "the testimony." LOGOS: "the testimony." PNEUMA: "the covenant tablets." Hebrew: ha-edut ("the testimony/witness"). Consensus: "the testimony" — ha-edut is the established term for the two tablets of stone stored in the ark (Exod 25:16); "testimony" is the standard translation; PNEUMA's "covenant tablets" is an interpretive expansion not in the Hebrew.

**V. 15 — "inside the veil" vs. "behind the curtain."** Same ruling as v. 12. Consensus: "inside the veil."

**V. 16 — "uncleannesses" vs. "ritual impurities" vs. "impurity."** EMET: "uncleannesses." LOGOS: "uncleannesses." PNEUMA: "ritual impurities" / "impurity." Hebrew: tum'ot ("uncleannesses"). Consensus: "uncleannesses" — "unclean" / "uncleanness" is the project standard for tame/tum'ah throughout Leviticus (cf. project convention: tameh/tahor = "unclean"/"clean"). PNEUMA's "ritual impurities" adds "ritual" as a qualifier not in the text; the MT does not distinguish ritual from moral impurity at the level of the word itself.

**V. 16 — "transgressions, all their sins" — three-term sequence.** EMET and LOGOS: "uncleannesses... transgressions... sins" (three distinct terms: tum'ot / pesha'im / chatot). PNEUMA: "ritual impurities... acts of rebellion... sins." Hebrew: tum'ot bney yisrael u-mipsha'eihem le-khol chatotam. Consensus preserves all three terms: "uncleannesses... transgressions... all their sins." PNEUMA's "acts of rebellion" for pesha is defensible but the more traditional "transgressions" is consistent with Leviticus project usage.

**V. 17 — "no man" vs. "no one."** EMET: "no man." LOGOS: "no man." PNEUMA: "no one." Hebrew: ve-khol-adam lo' ("and no man/person not"). Consensus: "No man" — adam is the generic term for "human" in Hebrew (not ish, the specifically masculine term); "no man" as a generic is traditional and consistent with LOGOS's rendering throughout Leviticus.

**V. 17 — scope of atonement list.** EMET: "himself and for his household and for all the congregation of Israel." LOGOS: "himself and for his house and for all the assembly of Israel." PNEUMA: "himself, his household, and the whole assembled community of Israel." Hebrew: kahal yisrael ("assembly/congregation of Israel"). Consensus: "himself and for his household and for all the congregation of Israel" — "congregation" for qahal is consistent with Leviticus project usage; "assembly" (LOGOS) is also acceptable; PNEUMA's "assembled community" is an expansion.

**V. 18 — "horns of the altar all around."** EMET: "the horns of the altar all around." LOGOS: "the horns of the altar all around." PNEUMA: "all four horns of the altar." Hebrew: al-qarnotav saviv ("on its horns all around"). Consensus: "the horns of the altar all around" — "all four" (PNEUMA) specifies the number; the MT says "all around" (saviv) which implies all horns without specifying four; "all around" is the more faithful rendering.

**V. 21 — "ish itti" — "a man who is in readiness" vs. "a man appointed for the task."** EMET: "he shall send it away into the wilderness" (EMET does not render ish itti explicitly in the clean extraction; the full text reads "by the hand of a ready man"). LOGOS: "by the hand of a man who is in readiness." PNEUMA: "in the care of a man appointed for the task." Hebrew: be-yad ish itti (ish = "man"; itti = of disputed meaning: "ready, appointed, designated"). Consensus: "by the hand of a man who is in readiness" — LOGOS's rendering follows the LXX (ἑτοίμου ἀνθρώπου, "a ready man") and preserves the ambiguity of the hapax legomenon itti. PNEUMA's "appointed for the task" is interpretively reasonable but goes beyond the text.

**V. 21 — "iniquities... transgressions... sins" three-term sequence.** All three agents render the three terms. EMET: "iniquities... transgressions... sins." LOGOS: "iniquities... transgressions... sins." PNEUMA: "iniquities... acts of rebellion... sins." Hebrew: avonot... pesha'im... chatot. Consensus: "iniquities... transgressions... all their sins" — the three terms are a deliberate comprehensive catalogue; "transgressions" (EMET, LOGOS) is the project standard for pesha.

**V. 22 — "a land cut off" vs. "a remote land" vs. "a desolate place."** EMET: "a land cut off." LOGOS: "a remote land." PNEUMA: "a desolate place." Hebrew: eretz gezeirah ("a land of cutting off / a cut-off land / a land of separation"). Consensus: "a land cut off" — gezeirah comes from the root gazar ("to cut, to cut off") and means "a land that is cut off, separated, isolated" — not merely remote but severed from the inhabited world. EMET's "a land cut off" is the most faithful; LOGOS's "remote land" loses the active-separation sense; PNEUMA's "desolate place" softens the imagery of being cut off/severed.

**V. 24 — "his garments" vs. "regular garments."** EMET: "his garments." LOGOS: "his garments." PNEUMA: "his regular garments." Hebrew: bigdav ("his garments"). Consensus: "his garments" — "regular" (PNEUMA) adds a clarifying gloss but it is not in the Hebrew. The contrast with the special linen garments is clear from context without adding "regular."

**V. 26 — "the one who sends away the goat for Azazel" vs. "he who releases the goat for Azazel."** EMET: "the one who sends away the goat for Azazel." LOGOS: "he who releases the goat for Azazel." PNEUMA: "The man who released the Azazel goat." Hebrew: ha-meshallach et-ha-sa'ir la-Azazel. Consensus: "the one who sends away the goat for Azazel" — EMET preserves the piel participle of shalach ("to send away/dispatch") and the standard "for Azazel" prepositional phrase; PNEUMA's "the Azazel goat" as a compound is slightly colloquial; LOGOS's "releases" is a defensible rendering but "sends away" better captures the dispatching act.

**V. 27 — "skin" vs. "hides."** EMET: "their skins." LOGOS: "their skin." PNEUMA: "their hides." Hebrew: et-orotehem ("their skin/hides"). Consensus: "their skin" — the singular collective is common in Hebrew; "skin" is traditional; "hides" (PNEUMA) is acceptable.

**V. 29 — "a statute forever" vs. "a permanent statute."** EMET: "a perpetual statute." LOGOS: "a statute forever." PNEUMA: "a permanent statute." Hebrew: le-chuqat olam ("as a statute of eternity"). Consensus: "a statute forever" — LOGOS's rendering matches the chapter's own three-time use of the formula (vv. 29, 31, 34) and the standard Levitical rendering of le-chuqat olam throughout the book; "perpetual" (EMET) and "permanent" (PNEUMA) are near-synonyms but "forever" captures olam most directly.

**V. 29 — "afflict yourselves" vs. "humble yourselves."** EMET: "you shall afflict your souls." LOGOS: "you shall afflict yourselves." PNEUMA: "you shall humble yourselves." Hebrew: ve-innitem et-nafshoteikhem (Piel of anah II + "your souls"). Consensus: "you shall afflict yourselves" — see Council Notes above. The Piel of anah II with "souls/selves" is the technical Levitical self-denial formula; "afflict" preserves its full range including bodily discipline; "humble" (PNEUMA / LXX ταπεινόω) captures the attitudinal dimension but is narrower. PNEUMA here follows the LXX.

**V. 29 — "native" vs. "native-born" and "sojourner" vs. "foreigner."** EMET: "the native and the sojourner." LOGOS: "the native and the sojourner." PNEUMA: "the native-born nor the foreigner." Hebrew: ha-ezrach ve-ha-ger. Consensus: "the native and the sojourner" — "sojourner" (EMET, LOGOS) is the standard project rendering for ger (a resident alien, someone dwelling among Israel but not of Israelite birth); "foreigner" (PNEUMA) is too strong — a ger is not merely foreign but a settled resident; "sojourner" preserves the distinction from both full Israelite (ezrach) and complete outsider (nokri).

**V. 31 — "sabbath of solemn rest" vs. "sabbath of complete rest."** EMET: "a sabbath of solemn rest." LOGOS: "a sabbath of solemn rest [shabbat shabbaton]." PNEUMA: "a sabbath of complete rest." Hebrew: shabbat shabbaton. Consensus: "a sabbath of solemn rest [shabbat shabbaton]" — the doubled intensive noun (shabbat + its abstract intensive shabbaton) is the superlative Sabbath formula; "solemn rest" is the established English translation (ESV/NASB tradition) that the project adopts with the bracketed transliteration.

**V. 32 — "consecrated to serve as priest" vs. "ordained."** EMET: "consecrated to minister as priest." LOGOS: "consecrated to serve as priest." PNEUMA: "ordained to serve." Hebrew: u-milleh et-yado lekhahein ("who fills his hand to serve as priest" — the ordination idiom). Consensus: "consecrated to serve as priest" — LOGOS's rendering of this idiomatic phrase is adopted; "ordained" (PNEUMA) is the functional equivalent but abandons the specific consecration-language of the text.

**V. 33 — "holy sanctuary" vs. "Most Holy Place" (PNEUMA).** EMET: "the holy sanctuary." LOGOS: "the holy sanctuary." PNEUMA: "the Most Holy Place." Hebrew: miqdashe ha-qodesh ("the sanctuary of holiness / the holy sanctuary"). Consensus: "the holy sanctuary" — miqdashe ha-qodesh refers to the sanctuary as a whole (all the sacred precincts); PNEUMA's "Most Holy Place" narrows it to only the inner chamber, which is inconsistent with the summary function of v. 33 (which lists sanctuary + tent of meeting + altar as three distinct items).

**V. 34 — "And Moses did" vs. "And it was done."** EMET: "and he did as the LORD commanded Moshe." LOGOS: "and Moses did as the LORD commanded him." PNEUMA: "And it was done as the LORD commanded Moses." Hebrew: va-ya'as ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Mosheh ("and he did as the LORD commanded Moses"). Consensus: "And Moses did as the LORD commanded him" — LOGOS. The subject of va-ya'as is implied; most interpreters take Aaron as the implicit subject, though the verse grammatically could point to Moses. LOGOS's standard priestly-close formula is the most natural rendering. PNEUMA's passive "it was done" is possible but erases the personal subject.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**On the kapporet and hilasterion (v. 2, 14):** The LXX's choice of ἱλαστήριον (hilasterion) for kapporet achieves independent theological weight in the New Testament. Paul uses the exact term in Romans 3:25 to describe Christ: "whom God put forward as a hilasterion by his blood." Greek-speaking readers of Paul's letters who knew Leviticus 16 through the LXX would have immediately understood: Christ is the new kapporet, the place where divine presence meets human guilt and blood is applied for atonement. The Day of Atonement is not merely an analogy Paul reaches for — it is the specific ritual his language evokes, word for word.

**On the two-goat theology (vv. 7–10, 20–22):** The two-goat ritual accomplishes together what no single image can achieve: the first goat (slaughtered, blood inside the veil) addresses guilt as a contamination of the sanctuary — where God dwells must be purged. The second goat (alive, confessed over, sent away) addresses guilt as a burden on the people — what they carry must be removed and taken far away. Substitutionary death and complete removal. The writer of Hebrews exploits both aspects, arguing that Christ performs both roles finally and permanently (Heb 9:11–14, 25–26): his blood enters the true Most Holy Place, and his sacrifice, once-offered, renders the annual cycle complete.

**On Azazel (vv. 8, 10, 26):** The syntactic parallel "one lot for the LORD and one lot for Azazel" (v. 8) places the two in grammatical equivalence — the most theologically uncomfortable feature of the text. All three agents, and the consensus, preserve the term untranslated. The LXX avoided this parallel by rendering ἀποπομπαῖος ("the sent-away one"), effectively denying Azazel proper-name status. The MT text preserves the ambiguity; the translation should do likewise.

**On "afflict yourselves" and Yom Kippur practice (vv. 29, 31):** The Talmud (b.Yoma 73b) enumerates five self-denials for Yom Kippur: eating/drinking, bathing, anointing, wearing leather sandals, and marital relations. The Hebrew ve-innitem et-nafshoteikhem encompasses all five; none of them except the first requires "humble" as a translation. The LXX's ταπεινώσετε τὰς ψυχὰς ὑμῶν ("you shall humble your souls") influenced subsequent Christian penitential theology toward an interior-attitudinal reading; the MT preserves a broader bodily-spiritual discipline.

**On the three-term sin catalogue (vv. 16, 21):** The chapter twice employs the three-term catalogue: tum'ot (uncleannesses/impurities), pesha'im (transgressions/rebellions), chatot (sins). These three terms together form a comprehensive taxonomy: involuntary ritual impurity (tum'ah), deliberate moral rebellion (pesha), and the general category of failure (chatah). The Day of Atonement addresses all three simultaneously. The LXX (Vaticanus) reduces the three-term list to two terms in v. 16 (ἀπὸ πασῶν τῶν ἁμαρτιῶν αὐτῶν, "from all their sins") but preserves the three-fold distinction in v. 21.

**On structural geography:** The chapter's atonement arc follows a centripetal-then-centrifugal geometry: blood drawn inward to the innermost room (vv. 11–14); then sin driven outward to the wilderness (vv. 20–22); then carcasses carried outside the camp (v. 27). Everything moves either deeper in or further out. The high priest's entire journey maps this same axis — from the outer altar, through the Holy Place, to the Holy of Holies, and back out again. The geometry of atonement is inward for the blood, outward for the sin.
